data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse of the copper‑production system in Cyprus eliminated a key economic engine, causing a chain reaction of economic decline throughout the trade‑dependent palace societies.", "mechanism": "Loss of copper exports led to loss of elite revenues, reduced ability to fund military and monument projects, and triggered internal unrest.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit"], "excludes": ["Levantine city‑states that relied less on copper", "Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Late Bronze Age records from Hattusa showing continued receipt of copper shipments after 1150 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "An economic‑centric explanation that downplays direct warfare; it is less common but offers a mechanistic view linking resource networks to collapse."}
